Along with Carl Dittman (also known as "Charley Brown"), George Nidever was a central figure in the 1853 party of white men and "Mission Indians" (likely Chumash and/or Gabrielino men) that ultimately brought the Lone Woman to the California mainland. After the ship’s return to Santa Barbara, the Lone Woman stayed in the home of Nidever and his wife, Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez de Nidever, until her death seven weeks later. Nidever, then, is an eyewitness to the Lone Woman’s habitation on San Nicolas Island, release from solitude, and life and death in Santa Barbara.
 

 Because he was the ship owner and captain of the 1853 sea otter hunting crew, and because he later hosted the Lone Woman in his home, Nidever has generally been credited with the Lone Woman’s "discovery" and "rescue." When the Santa Barbara chapter of the Daughters of the American Revolution erected a plaque on the site of the Lone Woman’s presumed burial at the Santa Barbara Mission, for example, it was inscribed: "Juana Maria/ Indian woman abandoned on/ San Nicolas Island eighteen years/ found and brought to/ Santa Barbara/ by/ Capt. George Nidever/ in 1853." In fact, however, it was Dittman who first saw and interacted with the woman. Regardless, Nidever and Dittman’s recorded recollections of the party’s initial encounter with the Lone Woman are similar. 
 

 In the last quarter of the nineteenth century, historian Hubert Howe Bancroft (for whom the University of California’s library is named) hired a number of research assistants to interview and take dictations from leading California figures. Both Nidever and Dittman contributed to this project by telling their life stories to Edward F. Murray in 1878, twenty-five years after the Lone Woman’s removal from San Nicolas. Nidever’s memoir was later published in a popular volume, The Life and Adventures of George Nidever: The Life Story of a Remarkable California Pioneer Told in his Own Words, and None Wasted (1937).
 

 The Life and Adventures of George Nidever not only provides an eyewitness account of the Lone Woman’s removal, albeit at a distance of a quarter century, but also offers contemporary readers insight into the California—and United States—that the Lone Woman entered. (Nidever relates the woman’s shock upon seeing an ox-cart and horses, for example, and reports that he received an offer, which he summarily refused, to display the Lone Woman in a side show, for profit). Importantly, however, the memoir also helps us to see—and reflect on— how Nidever made sense of the Lone Woman. He asserted that he understood her "plainly;" yet, from the distance of the twenty-first century, we can see how profound barriers of language, culture, and gender separated Nidever from the island woman.
 

 Nidever was born in eastern Tennessee in1802 and moved steadily west during his lifetime as a hunter, trapper, and rancher who also engaged in gold mining, land speculation, and occasional Indian fighting. In short, Nidever was a consummate pioneer. Born two generations after Daniel Boone, however, his landscape was California rather than Kentucky. Nidever arrived in California in 1833, having traveled from Arkansas with a party of trappers. Once on the west coast, he decided to remain in California, which was then part of Mexico (note the name of the woman he married: Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez). When California entered the union as the thirty-first state in 1850, Nidever became an "original" or "native" Californian.
 

 Nidever and the Lone Woman’s paths crossed because Nidever’s economic activities regularly took him to the loosely-regulated California islands: he hunted otter, collected seagull eggs, and, on San Miguel, operated a sheep ranch. Indigenous men frequently accompanied Nidever on these money-making expeditions, and the language Nidever employed in dictating his life story gives us clues as to the hierarchy involved in these relationships. Repeatedly, Nidever omits Native people’s names and uses possessive phrases such as "my Indian" to describe men with whom he worked, and on occasion, considered friends. The search party for the Lone Woman is a case in point: Nidever records that Charley Brown accompanied him on the trip, and in fact saw the Lone Woman first. But he neglects to name the "two Indians [who] went ashore" with them. Their identities remain unrecorded today, although the ethnographic notes of J. P. Harrington suggest some possibilities (e.g., the Chumash men Malquiares and Policarpio and the Gabrielino Nolverto). In stark contrast, early twentieth-century historians ensured the production of a memoir that secures Nidever’s name, and story, for perpetuity. In a very real sense, then, Nidever’s history rests on the erasure of Native peoples’ history.
 

 As The Life and Adventures of George Nidever records, Father González of the Santa Barbara Mission requested that Nidever and his hunting party search for the Lone Woman when on San Nicolas. Nidever went to some effort and expense to do so, as the journey to the most remote of the Channel Islands was more than 120 miles roundtrip in his schooner. It was on the third trip that Nidever finally succeeded. At that time, the Lone Woman had endured eighteen years of solitude; her "rescuers" don’t rush her to society, however. Rather, as Nidever reports, "we remained here hunting about a month." Their first goal, of course, was to make the expedition profitable.
 

 Today, Nidever is recognized as a pioneer of California and is perhaps best known as the "discoverer" of the Lone Woman. In all his years hunting, tracking, and trapping, one could say that she was his "biggest catch."

Along with Carl Dittman (also known as "Charley Brown"), George Nidever was a central figure in the 1853 party of white men and "Mission Indians" (likely Chumash and/or Gabrielino men) that ultimately brought the Lone Woman to the California mainland. After the ship’s return to Santa Barbara, the Lone Woman stayed in the home of Nidever and his wife, Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez de Nidever, until her death seven weeks later. Nidever, then, is an eyewitness to the Lone Woman’s habitation on San Nicolas Island, release from solitude, and life and death in Santa Barbara.
 

 Because he was the ship owner and captain of the 1853 sea otter hunting crew, and because he later hosted the Lone Woman in his home, Nidever has generally been credited with the Lone Woman’s "discovery" and "rescue." When the Santa Barbara chapter of the Daughters of the American Revolution erected a plaque on the site of the Lone Woman’s presumed burial at the Santa Barbara Mission, for example, it was inscribed: "Juana Maria/ Indian woman abandoned on/ San Nicolas Island eighteen years/ found and brought to/ Santa Barbara/ by/ Capt. George Nidever/ in 1853." In fact, however, it was Dittman who first saw and interacted with the woman. Regardless, Nidever and Dittman’s recorded recollections of the party’s initial encounter with the Lone Woman are similar. 
 

 In the last quarter of the nineteenth century, historian Hubert Howe Bancroft (for whom the University of California’s library is named) hired a number of research assistants to interview and take dictations from leading California figures. Both Nidever and Dittman contributed to this project by telling their life stories to Edward F. Murray in 1878, twenty-five years after the Lone Woman’s removal from San Nicolas. Nidever’s memoir was later published in a popular volume, The Life and Adventures of George Nidever: The Life Story of a Remarkable California Pioneer Told in his Own Words, and None Wasted (1937).
 

 The Life and Adventures of George Nidever not only provides an eyewitness account of the Lone Woman’s removal, albeit at a distance of a quarter century, but also offers contemporary readers insight into the California—and United States—that the Lone Woman entered. (Nidever relates the woman’s shock upon seeing an ox-cart and horses, for example, and reports that he received an offer, which he summarily refused, to display the Lone Woman in a side show, for profit). Importantly, however, the memoir also helps us to see—and reflect on— how Nidever made sense of the Lone Woman. He asserted that he understood her "plainly;" yet, from the distance of the twenty-first century, we can see how profound barriers of language, culture, and gender separated Nidever from the island woman.
 

 Nidever was born in eastern Tennessee in1802 and moved steadily west during his lifetime as a hunter, trapper, and rancher who also engaged in gold mining, land speculation, and occasional Indian fighting. In short, Nidever was a consummate pioneer. Born two generations after Daniel Boone, however, his landscape was California rather than Kentucky. Nidever arrived in California in 1833, having traveled from Arkansas with a party of trappers. Once on the west coast, he decided to remain in California, which was then part of Mexico (note the name of the woman he married: Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez). When California entered the union as the thirty-first state in 1850, Nidever became an "original" or "native" Californian.
 

 Nidever and the Lone Woman’s paths crossed because Nidever’s economic activities regularly took him to the loosely-regulated California islands: he hunted otter, collected seagull eggs, and, on San Miguel, operated a sheep ranch. Indigenous men frequently accompanied Nidever on these money-making expeditions, and the language Nidever employed in dictating his life story gives us clues as to the hierarchy involved in these relationships. Repeatedly, Nidever omits Native people’s names and uses possessive phrases such as "my Indian" to describe men with whom he worked, and on occasion, considered friends. The search party for the Lone Woman is a case in point: Nidever records that Charley Brown accompanied him on the trip, and in fact saw the Lone Woman first. But he neglects to name the "two Indians [who] went ashore" with them. Their identities remain unrecorded today, although the ethnographic notes of J. P. Harrington suggest some possibilities (e.g., the Chumash men Malquiares and Policarpio and the Gabrielino Nolverto). In stark contrast, early twentieth-century historians ensured the production of a memoir that secures Nidever’s name, and story, for perpetuity. In a very real sense, then, Nidever’s history rests on the erasure of Native peoples’ history.
 

 As The Life and Adventures of George Nidever records, Father González of the Santa Barbara Mission requested that Nidever and his hunting party search for the Lone Woman when on San Nicolas. Nidever went to some effort and expense to do so, as the journey to the most remote of the Channel Islands was more than 120 miles roundtrip in his schooner. It was on the third trip that Nidever finally succeeded. At that time, the Lone Woman had endured eighteen years of solitude; her "rescuers" don’t rush her to society, however. Rather, as Nidever reports, "we remained here hunting about a month." Their first goal, of course, was to make the expedition profitable.
 

 Today, Nidever is recognized as a pioneer of California and is perhaps best known as the "discoverer" of the Lone Woman. In all his years hunting, tracking, and trapping, one could say that she was his "biggest catch."

Along with Carl Dittman (also known as "Charley Brown"), George Nidever was a central figure in the 1853 party of white men and "Mission Indians" (likely Chumash and/or Gabrielino men) that ultimately brought the Lone Woman to the California mainland. After the ship’s return to Santa Barbara, the Lone Woman stayed in the home of Nidever and his wife, Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez de Nidever, until her death seven weeks later. Nidever, then, is an eyewitness to the Lone Woman’s habitation on San Nicolas Island, release from solitude, and life and death in Santa Barbara.
 

 Because he was the ship owner and captain of the 1853 sea otter hunting crew, and because he later hosted the Lone Woman in his home, Nidever has generally been credited with the Lone Woman’s "discovery" and "rescue." When the Santa Barbara chapter of the Daughters of the American Revolution erected a plaque on the site of the Lone Woman’s presumed burial at the Santa Barbara Mission, for example, it was inscribed: "Juana Maria/ Indian woman abandoned on/ San Nicolas Island eighteen years/ found and brought to/ Santa Barbara/ by/ Capt. George Nidever/ in 1853." In fact, however, it was Dittman who first saw and interacted with the woman. Regardless, Nidever and Dittman’s recorded recollections of the party’s initial encounter with the Lone Woman are similar. 
 

 In the last quarter of the nineteenth century, historian Hubert Howe Bancroft (for whom the University of California’s library is named) hired a number of research assistants to interview and take dictations from leading California figures. Both Nidever and Dittman contributed to this project by telling their life stories to Edward F. Murray in 1878, twenty-five years after the Lone Woman’s removal from San Nicolas. Nidever’s memoir was later published in a popular volume, The Life and Adventures of George Nidever: The Life Story of a Remarkable California Pioneer Told in his Own Words, and None Wasted (1937).
 

 The Life and Adventures of George Nidever not only provides an eyewitness account of the Lone Woman’s removal, albeit at a distance of a quarter century, but also offers contemporary readers insight into the California—and United States—that the Lone Woman entered. (Nidever relates the woman’s shock upon seeing an ox-cart and horses, for example, and reports that he received an offer, which he summarily refused, to display the Lone Woman in a side show, for profit). Importantly, however, the memoir also helps us to see—and reflect on— how Nidever made sense of the Lone Woman. He asserted that he understood her "plainly;" yet, from the distance of the twenty-first century, we can see how profound barriers of language, culture, and gender separated Nidever from the island woman.
 

 Nidever was born in eastern Tennessee in1802 and moved steadily west during his lifetime as a hunter, trapper, and rancher who also engaged in gold mining, land speculation, and occasional Indian fighting. In short, Nidever was a consummate pioneer. Born two generations after Daniel Boone, however, his landscape was California rather than Kentucky. Nidever arrived in California in 1833, having traveled from Arkansas with a party of trappers. Once on the west coast, he decided to remain in California, which was then part of Mexico (note the name of the woman he married: Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez). When California entered the union as the thirty-first state in 1850, Nidever became an "original" or "native" Californian.
 

 Nidever and the Lone Woman’s paths crossed because Nidever’s economic activities regularly took him to the loosely-regulated California islands: he hunted otter, collected seagull eggs, and, on San Miguel, operated a sheep ranch. Indigenous men frequently accompanied Nidever on these money-making expeditions, and the language Nidever employed in dictating his life story gives us clues as to the hierarchy involved in these relationships. Repeatedly, Nidever omits Native people’s names and uses possessive phrases such as "my Indian" to describe men with whom he worked, and on occasion, considered friends. The search party for the Lone Woman is a case in point: Nidever records that Charley Brown accompanied him on the trip, and in fact saw the Lone Woman first. But he neglects to name the "two Indians [who] went ashore" with them. Their identities remain unrecorded today, although the ethnographic notes of J. P. Harrington suggest some possibilities (e.g., the Chumash men Malquiares and Policarpio and the Gabrielino Nolverto). In stark contrast, early twentieth-century historians ensured the production of a memoir that secures Nidever’s name, and story, for perpetuity. In a very real sense, then, Nidever’s history rests on the erasure of Native peoples’ history.
 

 As The Life and Adventures of George Nidever records, Father González of the Santa Barbara Mission requested that Nidever and his hunting party search for the Lone Woman when on San Nicolas. Nidever went to some effort and expense to do so, as the journey to the most remote of the Channel Islands was more than 120 miles roundtrip in his schooner. It was on the third trip that Nidever finally succeeded. At that time, the Lone Woman had endured eighteen years of solitude; her "rescuers" don’t rush her to society, however. Rather, as Nidever reports, "we remained here hunting about a month." Their first goal, of course, was to make the expedition profitable.
 

 Today, Nidever is recognized as a pioneer of California and is perhaps best known as the "discoverer" of the Lone Woman. In all his years hunting, tracking, and trapping, one could say that she was his "biggest catch."

Along with Carl Dittman (also known as "Charley Brown"), George Nidever was a central figure in the 1853 party of white men and "Mission Indians" (likely Chumash and/or Gabrielino men) that ultimately brought the Lone Woman to the California mainland. After the ship’s return to Santa Barbara, the Lone Woman stayed in the home of Nidever and his wife, Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez de Nidever, until her death seven weeks later. Nidever, then, is an eyewitness to the Lone Woman’s habitation on San Nicolas Island, release from solitude, and life and death in Santa Barbara.
 

 Because he was the ship owner and captain of the 1853 sea otter hunting crew, and because he later hosted the Lone Woman in his home, Nidever has generally been credited with the Lone Woman’s "discovery" and "rescue." When the Santa Barbara chapter of the Daughters of the American Revolution erected a plaque on the site of the Lone Woman’s presumed burial at the Santa Barbara Mission, for example, it was inscribed: "Juana Maria/ Indian woman abandoned on/ San Nicolas Island eighteen years/ found and brought to/ Santa Barbara/ by/ Capt. George Nidever/ in 1853." In fact, however, it was Dittman who first saw and interacted with the woman. Regardless, Nidever and Dittman’s recorded recollections of the party’s initial encounter with the Lone Woman are similar. 
 

 In the last quarter of the nineteenth century, historian Hubert Howe Bancroft (for whom the University of California’s library is named) hired a number of research assistants to interview and take dictations from leading California figures. Both Nidever and Dittman contributed to this project by telling their life stories to Edward F. Murray in 1878, twenty-five years after the Lone Woman’s removal from San Nicolas. Nidever’s memoir was later published in a popular volume, The Life and Adventures of George Nidever: The Life Story of a Remarkable California Pioneer Told in his Own Words, and None Wasted (1937).
 

 The Life and Adventures of George Nidever not only provides an eyewitness account of the Lone Woman’s removal, albeit at a distance of a quarter century, but also offers contemporary readers insight into the California—and United States—that the Lone Woman entered. (Nidever relates the woman’s shock upon seeing an ox-cart and horses, for example, and reports that he received an offer, which he summarily refused, to display the Lone Woman in a side show, for profit). Importantly, however, the memoir also helps us to see—and reflect on— how Nidever made sense of the Lone Woman. He asserted that he understood her "plainly;" yet, from the distance of the twenty-first century, we can see how profound barriers of language, culture, and gender separated Nidever from the island woman.
 

 Nidever was born in eastern Tennessee in1802 and moved steadily west during his lifetime as a hunter, trapper, and rancher who also engaged in gold mining, land speculation, and occasional Indian fighting. In short, Nidever was a consummate pioneer. Born two generations after Daniel Boone, however, his landscape was California rather than Kentucky. Nidever arrived in California in 1833, having traveled from Arkansas with a party of trappers. Once on the west coast, he decided to remain in California, which was then part of Mexico (note the name of the woman he married: Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez). When California entered the union as the thirty-first state in 1850, Nidever became an "original" or "native" Californian.
 

 Nidever and the Lone Woman’s paths crossed because Nidever’s economic activities regularly took him to the loosely-regulated California islands: he hunted otter, collected seagull eggs, and, on San Miguel, operated a sheep ranch. Indigenous men frequently accompanied Nidever on these money-making expeditions, and the language Nidever employed in dictating his life story gives us clues as to the hierarchy involved in these relationships. Repeatedly, Nidever omits Native people’s names and uses possessive phrases such as "my Indian" to describe men with whom he worked, and on occasion, considered friends. The search party for the Lone Woman is a case in point: Nidever records that Charley Brown accompanied him on the trip, and in fact saw the Lone Woman first. But he neglects to name the "two Indians [who] went ashore" with them. Their identities remain unrecorded today, although the ethnographic notes of J. P. Harrington suggest some possibilities (e.g., the Chumash men Malquiares and Policarpio and the Gabrielino Nolverto). In stark contrast, early twentieth-century historians ensured the production of a memoir that secures Nidever’s name, and story, for perpetuity. In a very real sense, then, Nidever’s history rests on the erasure of Native peoples’ history.
 

 As The Life and Adventures of George Nidever records, Father González of the Santa Barbara Mission requested that Nidever and his hunting party search for the Lone Woman when on San Nicolas. Nidever went to some effort and expense to do so, as the journey to the most remote of the Channel Islands was more than 120 miles roundtrip in his schooner. It was on the third trip that Nidever finally succeeded. At that time, the Lone Woman had endured eighteen years of solitude; her "rescuers" don’t rush her to society, however. Rather, as Nidever reports, "we remained here hunting about a month." Their first goal, of course, was to make the expedition profitable.
 

 Today, Nidever is recognized as a pioneer of California and is perhaps best known as the "discoverer" of the Lone Woman. In all his years hunting, tracking, and trapping, one could say that she was his "biggest catch."

Along with Carl Dittman (also known as "Charley Brown"), George Nidever was a central figure in the 1853 party of white men and "Mission Indians" (likely Chumash and/or Gabrielino men) that ultimately brought the Lone Woman to the California mainland. After the ship’s return to Santa Barbara, the Lone Woman stayed in the home of Nidever and his wife, Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez de Nidever, until her death seven weeks later. Nidever, then, is an eyewitness to the Lone Woman’s habitation on San Nicolas Island, release from solitude, and life and death in Santa Barbara.
 

 Because he was the ship owner and captain of the 1853 sea otter hunting crew, and because he later hosted the Lone Woman in his home, Nidever has generally been credited with the Lone Woman’s "discovery" and "rescue." When the Santa Barbara chapter of the Daughters of the American Revolution erected a plaque on the site of the Lone Woman’s presumed burial at the Santa Barbara Mission, for example, it was inscribed: "Juana Maria/ Indian woman abandoned on/ San Nicolas Island eighteen years/ found and brought to/ Santa Barbara/ by/ Capt. George Nidever/ in 1853." In fact, however, it was Dittman who first saw and interacted with the woman. Regardless, Nidever and Dittman’s recorded recollections of the party’s initial encounter with the Lone Woman are similar. 
 

 In the last quarter of the nineteenth century, historian Hubert Howe Bancroft (for whom the University of California’s library is named) hired a number of research assistants to interview and take dictations from leading California figures. Both Nidever and Dittman contributed to this project by telling their life stories to Edward F. Murray in 1878, twenty-five years after the Lone Woman’s removal from San Nicolas. Nidever’s memoir was later published in a popular volume, The Life and Adventures of George Nidever: The Life Story of a Remarkable California Pioneer Told in his Own Words, and None Wasted (1937).
 

 The Life and Adventures of George Nidever not only provides an eyewitness account of the Lone Woman’s removal, albeit at a distance of a quarter century, but also offers contemporary readers insight into the California—and United States—that the Lone Woman entered. (Nidever relates the woman’s shock upon seeing an ox-cart and horses, for example, and reports that he received an offer, which he summarily refused, to display the Lone Woman in a side show, for profit). Importantly, however, the memoir also helps us to see—and reflect on— how Nidever made sense of the Lone Woman. He asserted that he understood her "plainly;" yet, from the distance of the twenty-first century, we can see how profound barriers of language, culture, and gender separated Nidever from the island woman.
 

 Nidever was born in eastern Tennessee in1802 and moved steadily west during his lifetime as a hunter, trapper, and rancher who also engaged in gold mining, land speculation, and occasional Indian fighting. In short, Nidever was a consummate pioneer. Born two generations after Daniel Boone, however, his landscape was California rather than Kentucky. Nidever arrived in California in 1833, having traveled from Arkansas with a party of trappers. Once on the west coast, he decided to remain in California, which was then part of Mexico (note the name of the woman he married: Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez). When California entered the union as the thirty-first state in 1850, Nidever became an "original" or "native" Californian.
 

 Nidever and the Lone Woman’s paths crossed because Nidever’s economic activities regularly took him to the loosely-regulated California islands: he hunted otter, collected seagull eggs, and, on San Miguel, operated a sheep ranch. Indigenous men frequently accompanied Nidever on these money-making expeditions, and the language Nidever employed in dictating his life story gives us clues as to the hierarchy involved in these relationships. Repeatedly, Nidever omits Native people’s names and uses possessive phrases such as "my Indian" to describe men with whom he worked, and on occasion, considered friends. The search party for the Lone Woman is a case in point: Nidever records that Charley Brown accompanied him on the trip, and in fact saw the Lone Woman first. But he neglects to name the "two Indians [who] went ashore" with them. Their identities remain unrecorded today, although the ethnographic notes of J. P. Harrington suggest some possibilities (e.g., the Chumash men Malquiares and Policarpio and the Gabrielino Nolverto). In stark contrast, early twentieth-century historians ensured the production of a memoir that secures Nidever’s name, and story, for perpetuity. In a very real sense, then, Nidever’s history rests on the erasure of Native peoples’ history.
 

 As The Life and Adventures of George Nidever records, Father González of the Santa Barbara Mission requested that Nidever and his hunting party search for the Lone Woman when on San Nicolas. Nidever went to some effort and expense to do so, as the journey to the most remote of the Channel Islands was more than 120 miles roundtrip in his schooner. It was on the third trip that Nidever finally succeeded. At that time, the Lone Woman had endured eighteen years of solitude; her "rescuers" don’t rush her to society, however. Rather, as Nidever reports, "we remained here hunting about a month." Their first goal, of course, was to make the expedition profitable.
 

 Today, Nidever is recognized as a pioneer of California and is perhaps best known as the "discoverer" of the Lone Woman. In all his years hunting, tracking, and trapping, one could say that she was his "biggest catch."

Along with Carl Dittman (also known as "Charley Brown"), George Nidever was a central figure in the 1853 party of white men and "Mission Indians" (likely Chumash and/or Gabrielino men) that ultimately brought the Lone Woman to the California mainland. After the ship’s return to Santa Barbara, the Lone Woman stayed in the home of Nidever and his wife, Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez de Nidever, until her death seven weeks later. Nidever, then, is an eyewitness to the Lone Woman’s habitation on San Nicolas Island, release from solitude, and life and death in Santa Barbara.
 

 Because he was the ship owner and captain of the 1853 sea otter hunting crew, and because he later hosted the Lone Woman in his home, Nidever has generally been credited with the Lone Woman’s "discovery" and "rescue." When the Santa Barbara chapter of the Daughters of the American Revolution erected a plaque on the site of the Lone Woman’s presumed burial at the Santa Barbara Mission, for example, it was inscribed: "Juana Maria/ Indian woman abandoned on/ San Nicolas Island eighteen years/ found and brought to/ Santa Barbara/ by/ Capt. George Nidever/ in 1853." In fact, however, it was Dittman who first saw and interacted with the woman. Regardless, Nidever and Dittman’s recorded recollections of the party’s initial encounter with the Lone Woman are similar. 
 

 In the last quarter of the nineteenth century, historian Hubert Howe Bancroft (for whom the University of California’s library is named) hired a number of research assistants to interview and take dictations from leading California figures. Both Nidever and Dittman contributed to this project by telling their life stories to Edward F. Murray in 1878, twenty-five years after the Lone Woman’s removal from San Nicolas. Nidever’s memoir was later published in a popular volume, The Life and Adventures of George Nidever: The Life Story of a Remarkable California Pioneer Told in his Own Words, and None Wasted (1937).
 

 The Life and Adventures of George Nidever not only provides an eyewitness account of the Lone Woman’s removal, albeit at a distance of a quarter century, but also offers contemporary readers insight into the California—and United States—that the Lone Woman entered. (Nidever relates the woman’s shock upon seeing an ox-cart and horses, for example, and reports that he received an offer, which he summarily refused, to display the Lone Woman in a side show, for profit). Importantly, however, the memoir also helps us to see—and reflect on— how Nidever made sense of the Lone Woman. He asserted that he understood her "plainly;" yet, from the distance of the twenty-first century, we can see how profound barriers of language, culture, and gender separated Nidever from the island woman.
 

 Nidever was born in eastern Tennessee in1802 and moved steadily west during his lifetime as a hunter, trapper, and rancher who also engaged in gold mining, land speculation, and occasional Indian fighting. In short, Nidever was a consummate pioneer. Born two generations after Daniel Boone, however, his landscape was California rather than Kentucky. Nidever arrived in California in 1833, having traveled from Arkansas with a party of trappers. Once on the west coast, he decided to remain in California, which was then part of Mexico (note the name of the woman he married: Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez). When California entered the union as the thirty-first state in 1850, Nidever became an "original" or "native" Californian.
 

 Nidever and the Lone Woman’s paths crossed because Nidever’s economic activities regularly took him to the loosely-regulated California islands: he hunted otter, collected seagull eggs, and, on San Miguel, operated a sheep ranch. Indigenous men frequently accompanied Nidever on these money-making expeditions, and the language Nidever employed in dictating his life story gives us clues as to the hierarchy involved in these relationships. Repeatedly, Nidever omits Native people’s names and uses possessive phrases such as "my Indian" to describe men with whom he worked, and on occasion, considered friends. The search party for the Lone Woman is a case in point: Nidever records that Charley Brown accompanied him on the trip, and in fact saw the Lone Woman first. But he neglects to name the "two Indians [who] went ashore" with them. Their identities remain unrecorded today, although the ethnographic notes of J. P. Harrington suggest some possibilities (e.g., the Chumash men Malquiares and Policarpio and the Gabrielino Nolverto). In stark contrast, early twentieth-century historians ensured the production of a memoir that secures Nidever’s name, and story, for perpetuity. In a very real sense, then, Nidever’s history rests on the erasure of Native peoples’ history.
 

 As The Life and Adventures of George Nidever records, Father González of the Santa Barbara Mission requested that Nidever and his hunting party search for the Lone Woman when on San Nicolas. Nidever went to some effort and expense to do so, as the journey to the most remote of the Channel Islands was more than 120 miles roundtrip in his schooner. It was on the third trip that Nidever finally succeeded. At that time, the Lone Woman had endured eighteen years of solitude; her "rescuers" don’t rush her to society, however. Rather, as Nidever reports, "we remained here hunting about a month." Their first goal, of course, was to make the expedition profitable.
 

 Today, Nidever is recognized as a pioneer of California and is perhaps best known as the "discoverer" of the Lone Woman. In all his years hunting, tracking, and trapping, one could say that she was his "biggest catch."

Along with Carl Dittman (also known as "Charley Brown"), George Nidever was a central figure in the 1853 party of white men and "Mission Indians" (likely Chumash and/or Gabrielino men) that ultimately brought the Lone Woman to the California mainland. After the ship’s return to Santa Barbara, the Lone Woman stayed in the home of Nidever and his wife, Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez de Nidever, until her death seven weeks later. Nidever, then, is an eyewitness to the Lone Woman’s habitation on San Nicolas Island, release from solitude, and life and death in Santa Barbara.
 

 Because he was the ship owner and captain of the 1853 sea otter hunting crew, and because he later hosted the Lone Woman in his home, Nidever has generally been credited with the Lone Woman’s "discovery" and "rescue." When the Santa Barbara chapter of the Daughters of the American Revolution erected a plaque on the site of the Lone Woman’s presumed burial at the Santa Barbara Mission, for example, it was inscribed: "Juana Maria/ Indian woman abandoned on/ San Nicolas Island eighteen years/ found and brought to/ Santa Barbara/ by/ Capt. George Nidever/ in 1853." In fact, however, it was Dittman who first saw and interacted with the woman. Regardless, Nidever and Dittman’s recorded recollections of the party’s initial encounter with the Lone Woman are similar. 
 

 In the last quarter of the nineteenth century, historian Hubert Howe Bancroft (for whom the University of California’s library is named) hired a number of research assistants to interview and take dictations from leading California figures. Both Nidever and Dittman contributed to this project by telling their life stories to Edward F. Murray in 1878, twenty-five years after the Lone Woman’s removal from San Nicolas. Nidever’s memoir was later published in a popular volume, The Life and Adventures of George Nidever: The Life Story of a Remarkable California Pioneer Told in his Own Words, and None Wasted (1937).
 

 The Life and Adventures of George Nidever not only provides an eyewitness account of the Lone Woman’s removal, albeit at a distance of a quarter century, but also offers contemporary readers insight into the California—and United States—that the Lone Woman entered. (Nidever relates the woman’s shock upon seeing an ox-cart and horses, for example, and reports that he received an offer, which he summarily refused, to display the Lone Woman in a side show, for profit). Importantly, however, the memoir also helps us to see—and reflect on— how Nidever made sense of the Lone Woman. He asserted that he understood her "plainly;" yet, from the distance of the twenty-first century, we can see how profound barriers of language, culture, and gender separated Nidever from the island woman.
 

 Nidever was born in eastern Tennessee in1802 and moved steadily west during his lifetime as a hunter, trapper, and rancher who also engaged in gold mining, land speculation, and occasional Indian fighting. In short, Nidever was a consummate pioneer. Born two generations after Daniel Boone, however, his landscape was California rather than Kentucky. Nidever arrived in California in 1833, having traveled from Arkansas with a party of trappers. Once on the west coast, he decided to remain in California, which was then part of Mexico (note the name of the woman he married: Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez). When California entered the union as the thirty-first state in 1850, Nidever became an "original" or "native" Californian.
 

 Nidever and the Lone Woman’s paths crossed because Nidever’s economic activities regularly took him to the loosely-regulated California islands: he hunted otter, collected seagull eggs, and, on San Miguel, operated a sheep ranch. Indigenous men frequently accompanied Nidever on these money-making expeditions, and the language Nidever employed in dictating his life story gives us clues as to the hierarchy involved in these relationships. Repeatedly, Nidever omits Native people’s names and uses possessive phrases such as "my Indian" to describe men with whom he worked, and on occasion, considered friends. The search party for the Lone Woman is a case in point: Nidever records that Charley Brown accompanied him on the trip, and in fact saw the Lone Woman first. But he neglects to name the "two Indians [who] went ashore" with them. Their identities remain unrecorded today, although the ethnographic notes of J. P. Harrington suggest some possibilities (e.g., the Chumash men Malquiares and Policarpio and the Gabrielino Nolverto). In stark contrast, early twentieth-century historians ensured the production of a memoir that secures Nidever’s name, and story, for perpetuity. In a very real sense, then, Nidever’s history rests on the erasure of Native peoples’ history.
 

 As The Life and Adventures of George Nidever records, Father González of the Santa Barbara Mission requested that Nidever and his hunting party search for the Lone Woman when on San Nicolas. Nidever went to some effort and expense to do so, as the journey to the most remote of the Channel Islands was more than 120 miles roundtrip in his schooner. It was on the third trip that Nidever finally succeeded. At that time, the Lone Woman had endured eighteen years of solitude; her "rescuers" don’t rush her to society, however. Rather, as Nidever reports, "we remained here hunting about a month." Their first goal, of course, was to make the expedition profitable.
 

 Today, Nidever is recognized as a pioneer of California and is perhaps best known as the "discoverer" of the Lone Woman. In all his years hunting, tracking, and trapping, one could say that she was his "biggest catch."

The memoir of George Nidever has long been considered a key source in documenting the Lone Woman’s story. Along with Carl Dittman (also known as "Charley Brown"), George Nidever was a central figure in the 1853 party of white men and "Mission Indians" (likely Chumash and/or Gabrielino men) that ultimately brought the Lone Woman to the California mainland. After the ship’s return to Santa Barbara, the Lone Woman stayed in the home of Nidever and his wife, Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez de Nidever, until her death seven weeks later. Nidever, then, is an eyewitness to the Lone Woman’s habitation on San Nicolas Island, release from solitude, and life and death in Santa Barbara.
 

 Because he was the ship owner and captain of the 1853 sea otter hunting crew, and because he later hosted the Lone Woman in his home, Nidever has generally been credited with the Lone Woman’s "discovery" and "rescue." When the Santa Barbara chapter of the Daughters of the American Revolution erected a plaque on the site of the Lone Woman’s presumed burial at the Santa Barbara Mission, for example, it was inscribed: "Juana Maria/ Indian woman abandoned on/ San Nicolas Island eighteen years/ found and brought to/ Santa Barbara/ by/ Capt. George Nidever/ in 1853." In fact, however, it was Dittman who first saw and interacted with the woman. Regardless, Nidever and Dittman’s recorded recollections of the party’s initial encounter with the Lone Woman are similar. 
 

 In the last quarter of the nineteenth century, historian Hubert Howe Bancroft (for whom the University of California’s library is named) hired a number of research assistants to interview and take dictations from leading California figures. Both Nidever and Dittman contributed to this project by telling their life stories to Edward F. Murray in 1878, twenty-five years after the Lone Woman’s removal from San Nicolas. Nidever’s memoir was later published in a popular volume, The Life and Adventures of George Nidever: The Life Story of a Remarkable California Pioneer Told in his Own Words, and None Wasted (1937).
 

 The Life and Adventures of George Nidever not only provides an eyewitness account of the Lone Woman’s removal, albeit at a distance of a quarter century, but also offers contemporary readers insight into the California—and United States—that the Lone Woman entered. (Nidever relates the woman’s shock upon seeing an ox-cart and horses, for example, and reports that he received an offer, which he summarily refused, to display the Lone Woman in a side show, for profit). Importantly, however, the memoir also helps us to see—and reflect on— how Nidever made sense of the Lone Woman. He asserted that he understood her "plainly;" yet, from the distance of the twenty-first century, we can see how profound barriers of language, culture, and gender separated Nidever from the island woman.
 

 Nidever was born in eastern Tennessee in1802 and moved steadily west during his lifetime as a hunter, trapper, and rancher who also engaged in gold mining, land speculation, and occasional Indian fighting. In short, Nidever was a consummate pioneer. Born two generations after Daniel Boone, however, his landscape was California rather than Kentucky. Nidever arrived in California in 1833, having traveled from Arkansas with a party of trappers. Once on the west coast, he decided to remain in California, which was then part of Mexico (note the name of the woman he married: Maria Sinforosa Ramona Sanchez). When California entered the union as the thirty-first state in 1850, Nidever became an "original" or "native" Californian.
 

 Nidever and the Lone Woman’s paths crossed because Nidever’s economic activities regularly took him to the loosely-regulated California islands: he hunted otter, collected seagull eggs, and, on San Miguel, operated a sheep ranch. Indigenous men frequently accompanied Nidever on these money-making expeditions, and the language Nidever employed in dictating his life story gives us clues as to the hierarchy involved in these relationships. Repeatedly, Nidever omits Native people’s names and uses possessive phrases such as "my Indian" to describe men with whom he worked, and on occasion, considered friends. The search party for the Lone Woman is a case in point: Nidever records that Charley Brown accompanied him on the trip, and in fact saw the Lone Woman first. But he neglects to name the "two Indians [who] went ashore" with them. Their identities remain unrecorded today, although the ethnographic notes of J. P. Harrington suggest some possibilities (e.g., the Chumash men Malquiares and Policarpio and the Gabrielino Nolverto). In stark contrast, early twentieth-century historians ensured the production of a memoir that secures Nidever’s name, and story, for perpetuity. In a very real sense, then, Nidever’s history rests on the erasure of Native peoples’ history.
 

 As The Life and Adventures of George Nidever records, Father González of the Santa Barbara Mission requested that Nidever and his hunting party search for the Lone Woman when on San Nicolas. Nidever went to some effort and expense to do so, as the journey to the most remote of the Channel Islands was more than 120 miles roundtrip in his schooner. It was on the third trip that Nidever finally succeeded. At that time, the Lone Woman had endured eighteen years of solitude; her "rescuers" don’t rush her to society, however. Rather, as Nidever reports, "we remained here hunting about a month." Their first goal, of course, was to make the expedition profitable.
 

 Today, Nidever is recognized as a pioneer of California and is perhaps best known as the "discoverer" of the Lone Woman. In all his years hunting, tracking, and trapping, one could say that she was his "biggest catch."

title: "A Female Crusoe"
tropes: Lazy Indian; Noble Savage; Jump Overboard; Indian Queen; Girl Crusoe; Wild Child
document_intro:
The story of the Lone Woman’s isolation on San Nicolas Island was first published in a Boston newspaper, not a California one, and it appeared in 1847, years before the Nicoleña’s 1853 arrival in Santa Barbara. News of the Lone Woman’s island isolation was undoubtedly carried to New England by the Boston men who figured prominently in the Northwest and China trades. They likely heard the tale from fellow sailors while anchored in a California port, and they then brought the story home to New England. In fact, we can see the story travel from California to Boston in the journal of William Dane Phelps. A seaman born in Gloucester, MA, and living in Boston, Phelps kept elaborate journals at sea, in part for the entertainment of his wife (he sometimes sent journal pages home in advance of his return, by way of Boston-bound ships). In May of 1841, Phelps recorded the story of the Lone Woman and her people, which he had heard from another captain (Thomas Robbins) on the California coast. Phelps might not be the source for the Boston Atlas story, but his journal provides clues as to how the tale initially reached Boston.  
 

 Once the Lone Woman’s story was told in a Boston paper, it appeared in New York the next day (a copy of the Boston Atlas likely having traveled south by train). Within a month, the story had spread through the mails, reaching rural South Carolina by February and Wisconsin by March, for example. Simultaneously, it continued to circulate in New England and New York. Editors often held an interesting story in reserve, waiting for a column gap of the right length to insert the waiting copy. Seven months after the "A Female Crusoe" initially appeared in the Boston Atlas, the story of the Lone Woman "doubled the horn" a second time; a direct reprint of the article appeared in the Honolulu Polynesian, likely carried to Hawaii in a newspaper taken aboard a ship outbound on the Pacific trade.  
 

 The Boston Atlas article and its reprints is notable for linking the Lone Woman to Robinson Crusoe in its headline and for describing the Nicoleños as Noble Savages, a peaceful people who were taken off-guard by the arrival of Alaska Native hunters from Kodiak Island (which the article’s author erroneously dates to "1818 or 1820" rather than 1814). In the article’s telling, some years after the Alaska Native hunters leave the island, a ship is sent from the mainland to collect the remaining Nicoleños. The Lone Woman boards the ship but takes a dramatic dive off of it, leaving behind a bereaved mate who will pine for her on the mainland for years to come. The Lone Woman is described as voluntarily choosing to remain on her natal island, where she is an Indian Queen, "the monarch of all she surveys." She wears a birdskin dress and flees when she encounters any hunters.

 Once the Lone Woman’s story was told in a Boston paper, it appeared in New York the next day (a copy of the Boston Atlas likely having traveled south by train). Within a month, the story had spread through the mails, reaching rural South Carolina by February and Wisconsin by March, for example. Simultaneously, it continued to circulate in New England and New York. Editors often held an interesting story in reserve, waiting for a column gap of the right length to insert the waiting copy. Seven months after the "A Female Crusoe" initially appeared in the Boston Atlas, the story of the Lone Woman "doubled the horn" a second time; a direct reprint of the article appeared in the Honolulu Polynesian, likely carried to Hawaii in a newspaper taken aboard a ship outbound on the Pacific trade.  
 

 The Boston Atlas article and its reprints is notable for linking the Lone Woman to Robinson Crusoe in its headline and for describing the Nicoleños as Noble Savages, a peaceful people who were taken off-guard by the arrival of Alaska Native hunters from Kodiak Island (which the article’s author erroneously dates to "1818 or 1820" rather than 1814). In the article’s telling, some years after the Alaska Native hunters leave the island, a ship is sent from the mainland to collect the remaining Nicoleños. The Lone Woman boards the ship but takes a dramatic dive off of it, leaving behind a bereaved mate who will pine for her on the mainland for years to come. The Lone Woman is described as voluntarily choosing to remain on her natal island, where she is an Indian Queen, "the monarch of all she surveys." She wears a birdskin dress and flees when she encounters any hunters.
